question: How do the four elements of the first angel's message (euangelion aionion + phobethete + dote doxan + hoti elthen he hora tes kriseos + proskynesate to poiesanti) function as one integrated gospel proclamation? How does Ecclesiastes 12:13-14 demonstrate this is the same message? How does Paul at Athens (Acts 17:22-31) serve as apostolic precedent — element by element? How does Psalm 96 contain every element in OT worship form? Capstone synthesizing studies 04-08.

topics.name: GLORIFYING GOD
topics.entry_text: Commanded: 1Ch 16:28; Psa 22:23; Isa 42:12. Due to him: 1Ch 16:29. For his judgments: Isa 25:3; Ezk 28:22; Rev 14:7. Accomplished by: Relying on his promises (Rom 4:20), Praising him (Psa 50:23), Doing all to glorify him (1Co 10:31), Dying for him (Jhn 21:19), Bringing forth fruits (Jhn 15:8), Faithfulness (1Pe 4:11). Required in body and spirit: 1Co 6:20. The wicked averse to: Dan 5:23; Rom 1:21. Punishment for not: Dan 5:23,30; Mal 2:2; Act 12:23; Rom 1:21. Exemplified by Jesus: Jhn 17:4. By Abraham: Rom 4:20.

topics.name: WORSHIP
topics.entry_text: To be rendered only to God: Exo 20:3; Deu 5:7; 6:13; Mat 4:10; Luk 4:8; Act 10:26; 14:15; Col 2:18; Rev 19:10; 22:8. Of angels, forbidden: Rev 19:10; 22:8,9.

topics.name: REPENTANCE
topics.entry_text: A complete reversal of one's attitude and values, a turning toward God. The burden of the preaching of John the Baptist (Mat 3), of Jesus (Mat 4:17; Mrk 1:15). Exhortation to: Pro 1:23-33; Jer 7:3,5; 26:3; Hos 14:1-3; Amo 5:4-6; Mat 3:2. Unclassified scriptures include Act 17:30.

topics.name: FAITH
topics.entry_text: General faith entry. Key verses for this study: Gen 15:6 (Abraham believed), Rom 4:20 (Abraham's faith as glory-giving), Rev 14:12 (faith of Jesus).

topics.name: PRAISE
topics.entry_text: Praise psalms containing new song, creation worship, and judgment themes. Psalm 96 (new song + glory + creation + judgment), Psalm 98 (new song + judgment), Psalm 33 (new song + creation by word + fear of the LORD).

topics.name: PREACHING
topics.entry_text: Includes the basar/euangelizo bridge: Rom 10:15 quoting Isa 52:7, Luke 4:18 quoting Isa 61:1, Gal 3:8 proeuangelizomai.

verses.heading: The first angel's message in full — the text being synthesized
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: Revelation 14:1-5 introduces the Lamb and 144,000 on Mount Zion singing a new song. Verses 6-7 are the first of three angelic proclamations positioned between the secured victory of 14:1-5 and the dual harvest of 14:14-20. The angel flies in midheaven with universal scope.
verses.notes: Three aorist imperatives (phobethete, dote, proskynesate) form a climactic triad: internal orientation (fear), relational acknowledgment (give glory), behavioral expression (worship). The legon participle structurally embeds v.7 as the CONTENT of the euangelion in v.6. The hoti clause grounds all three imperatives. The creation formula uses poieo (not ktizo), mapping to Exo 20:11 LXX. The anarthrous euangelion aionion is qualitative. The cognate accusative euangelion euangelisai intensifies the gospel-ness.

verses.heading: Commandments + faith of Jesus — gospel result completing the inclusio
verses.kjv_text: Here is the patience of the saints: here [are] they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: Follows the third angel's warning (14:9-11). Rev 14:12 is the conclusion of the three angels' messages, describing the character of those who respond rightly. Forms an inclusio with Rev 12:17.
verses.notes: The articular present participle hoi terountes (V-PAP-NPM) is substantival — it describes who the saints ARE, not what they must DO to be saved. Descriptive, not prescriptive. The pistin Iesou genitive can be objective (faith IN Jesus) or subjective (faithfulness OF Jesus). The commandment inclusio: Rev 12:17 (entolas tou theou) and Rev 14:12 (entolas tou theou) bracket the three angels' messages.

verses.heading: Opening bracket of the commandment inclusio
verses.kjv_text: And the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ.
verses.context: Concludes Revelation 12, the great cosmic conflict chapter. The dragon targets those who keep God's commandments and have the testimony of Jesus — the same two markers that reappear in 14:12.
verses.notes: Establishes the commandment-keeping + testimony/faith of Jesus profile that brackets the three angels' messages. The war against commandment-keepers in 12:17 is answered by the gospel call of 14:6-7 and the character description of 14:12.

verses.heading: OT structural twin — fear God + commandments + judgment = 'the whole of man'
verses.kjv_text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this [is] the whole [duty] of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether [it be] good, or whether [it be] evil.
verses.context: The conclusion of the book of Ecclesiastes. After Solomon's exploration of meaning and vanity, this is his final verdict on the purpose of human existence. The Hebrew kol-ha-adam has three interpretive options: 'all mankind,' 'whole duty of man,' or 'man's all' (entire purpose/being).
verses.notes: Element mapping to Rev 14:7,12: (1) 'Fear God' (yare et ha-elohim) = 'Fear God' (phobethete ton theon), (2) 'keep his commandments' (mitsvotav shamar) = 'commandments of God' (entolas tou theou, Rev 14:12), (3) 'God shall bring every work into judgment' (mishpat) = 'the hour of his judgment is come' (kriseos). LXX bridges verified: yare maps to phobeo (241 co-occurrences), mitsvah maps to entole (153 co-occurrences), mishpat maps to krisis. The kol-ha-adam being 'man's all' means Eccl 12:13 defines the totality of human existence — paralleling the 'everlasting gospel' as the comprehensive message for 'every nation.' Fifth iteration of the farewell-address pattern (Moses, Joshua, Samuel, Solomon, first angel).

verses.heading: Apostolic precedent — Paul's Athens sermon mapping element by element to Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: Then Paul stood in the midst of Mars' hill, and said, [Ye] men of Athens, I perceive that in all things ye are too superstitious. For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you. God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands; Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things; And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation; That they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him, and find him, though he be not far from every one of us: For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring. Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device. And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by [that] man whom he hath ordained; [whereof] he hath given assurance unto all [men], in that he hath raised him from the dead.
verses.context: Paul at the Areopagus, addressing Athenian philosophers. This is his gospel for pagans — people without OT background. Parsons identifies a chiastic structure: A (ignorant worship, 22-23) / B (true worship of Creator, 24-25) / C (humanity-God relationship, 26-28) / B' (critique of idol worship, 29) / A' (end of ignorance via judgment, 30-31).
verses.notes: Element-by-element mapping to Rev 14:7: (a) 'God who made (poiesas, G4160) the world' (17:24) = 'worship him that made (poiesanti, G4160)' (14:7) — SAME VERB poieo in both; (b) 'commands all people everywhere to repent (metanoein)' (17:30) = 'fear God and give him glory' (14:7) — repentance and fear as moral reorientation; (c) 'appointed a day (hemeran) to judge (krinein) the world' (17:31) = 'the hour (hora) of his judgment (kriseos) is come' (14:7); (d) 'does not dwell in temples made with hands (cheiropoietois)' (17:24) = worship correction away from false worship toward the Creator. Temporal shift: Acts 17:31 uses mellei (about to), Rev 14:7 uses elthen (IS come) — same message, different temporal phase. Carson: this framework is 'the necessary framework without which his proclamation of Jesus makes no sense.' Paul delays mentioning Christ until v.31, just as Rev 14:7 delays explicit christological naming until 14:12.

verses.heading: OT worship form containing every element of Rev 14:6-7
verses.kjv_text: O sing unto the LORD a new song: sing unto the LORD, all the earth. Sing unto the LORD, bless his name; shew forth his salvation from day to day. Declare his glory among the heathen, his wonders among all people. For the LORD [is] great, and greatly to be praised: he [is] to be feared above all gods. For all the gods of the nations [are] idols: but the LORD made the heavens. Honour and majesty [are] before him: strength and beauty [are] in his sanctuary. Give unto the LORD, O ye kindreds of the people, give unto the LORD glory and strength. Give unto the LORD the glory [due unto] his name: bring an offering, and come into his courts. O worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness: fear before him, all the earth. Say among the heathen [that] the LORD reigneth: the world also shall be established that it shall not be moved: he shall judge the people righteously. Let the heavens rejoice, and let the earth be glad; let the sea roar, and the fulness thereof. Let the field be joyful, and all that [is] therein: then shall all the trees of the wood rejoice Before the LORD: for he cometh, for he cometh to judge the earth: he shall judge the world with righteousness, and the people with his truth.
verses.context: An enthronement psalm celebrating YHWH's kingship over all nations. One of the 'new song' psalms. Contains every element of the first angel's message in OT worship form.
verses.notes: Element mapping: (1) 'Sing a new song' (v.1, shiyr chadash) connects to Rev 14:3's new song via LXX ode kaine; (2) 'shew forth his salvation' (v.2, basseru yeshuato — Piel of basar, 'proclaim good news of his salvation') = 'everlasting gospel' — basar is the Hebrew root behind euangelizo; (3) 'Declare his glory among the heathen' (v.3) = 'every nation and kindred' (Rev 14:6) — universal scope; (4) 'the LORD made (asah) the heavens' (v.5) = 'worship him that made (poieo = asah in LXX)' (Rev 14:7); (5) 'Give unto the LORD glory (kabod)' (vv.7-8) = 'give him glory (doxa)' (Rev 14:7); (6) 'worship the LORD' (v.9) = 'worship him that made' (Rev 14:7); (7) 'he cometh to judge (shaphat) the earth' (v.13) = 'the hour of his judgment is come' (Rev 14:7). Critical: v.5 in the Hebrew reads kol-elohe ha-ammim elilim va-YHWH shamayim asah — 'all the gods of the nations are worthless things, but the LORD made the heavens.' This is the creation-vs-idolatry antithesis that both Acts 17 and Rev 14 present.

verses.heading: Moses' farewell — fear + commandments + obedience pattern
verses.kjv_text: And now, Israel, what doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD thy God, to walk in all his ways, and to love him, and to serve the LORD thy God with all thy heart and with all thy soul, To keep the commandments of the LORD, and his statutes, which I command thee this day for thy good?
verses.context: Moses' farewell address to Israel before they enter the Promised Land. The first iteration of the farewell-address fear-commandments pattern.
verses.notes: Pattern: fear (yirah, H3372 Qal InfCon) + walk + love + serve (avod, H5647) + keep commandments (lishmor mitsvot, H8104 + H4687). First iteration of the pattern that will recur in Joshua, Samuel, Solomon, and the first angel.

verses.heading: Joshua's farewell — fear + serve + remove idols
verses.kjv_text: Now therefore fear the LORD, and serve him in sincerity and in truth: and put away the gods which your fathers served on the other side of the flood, and in Egypt; and serve ye the LORD.
verses.context: Joshua's farewell address at Shechem. Second iteration of the farewell-address pattern, adding the idol-removal element.
verses.notes: Pattern: fear (yeru, H3372 Qal Impv) + serve (ivdu, H5647 Qal Impv) in sincerity/truth + put away false gods (hasiru, H5493 Hiphil Impv). Adds the idol-removal dimension that appears in both Acts 17:29 and the creation-vs-idolatry contrast of Psa 96:5.

verses.heading: Samuel's farewell — fear + serve + remember God's acts
verses.kjv_text: Only fear the LORD, and serve him in truth with all your heart: for consider how great [things] he hath done for you.
verses.context: Samuel's farewell address after the appointment of Saul as king. Third iteration of the farewell-address pattern.
verses.notes: Pattern: fear (yeru, H3372 Qal Impv) + serve (avadtem, H5647) in truth + consider/remember (reu, H7200 Qal Impv) God's great acts. Samuel adds the 'remember what God has done' element — connecting to creation memory in Rev 14:7.

verses.heading: Paul labels judgment as 'according to my gospel'
verses.kjv_text: In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to my gospel.
verses.context: Romans 2:1-16 discusses God's righteous judgment. Paul identifies judgment day as part of his gospel proclamation — kata to euangelion mou ('according to MY gospel').
verses.notes: One of three independent NT texts labeling judgment as gospel. The articular to euangelion mou ('MY gospel') is Paul's own designation — he considers judgment content to be part of the gospel he preaches. The judgment is 'through Jesus Christ' (dia Christou Iesou), making it explicitly christological.

verses.heading: John's judgment preaching explicitly labeled euangelizo
verses.kjv_text: Whose fan [is] in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and will gather the wheat into his garner; but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable. And many other things in his exhortation preached he unto the people.
verses.context: Luke 3:7-17 records John the Baptist's judgment preaching — fire, axe at root, winnowing fork. Then v.18 labels this preaching 'euangelizeto' — 'he preached the gospel.'
verses.notes: Second independent text labeling judgment as gospel. Luke uses euangelizeto (imperfect middle of euangelizo) to label the entirety of John's preaching — including the judgment fire of v.17 — as gospel. If judgment preaching IS gospel when John does it, the same content in Rev 14:7 cannot be non-gospel.

verses.heading: Christ as Creator — christological content implicit in 'worship him that made'
verses.kjv_text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. The same was in the beginning with God. All things were made by him; and without him was not any thing made that was made.
verses.context: The prologue of John's Gospel identifying the Word (Logos) as the agent of creation. 'All things were made by him' establishes Christ as Creator.
verses.notes: When Rev 14:7 says 'worship him that made,' the 'him that made' includes Christ. The 'natural theology' objection ('no Christ, therefore no gospel') fails because Christ IS the Creator being invoked. The absence of explicit christological naming in Rev 14:7 is not the absence of Christ — it is the invocation of Christ under his Creator title.

verses.heading: Christ as Creator — 'by him were all things created'
verses.kjv_text: Who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature: For by him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, visible and invisible, whether [they be] thrones, or dominions, or principalities, or powers: all things were created by him, and for him: And he is before all things, and by him all things consist.
verses.context: The Colossian Christ-hymn establishing Christ's role in creation. Paul uses ktizo (created) here, but the theological point applies to Rev 14:7's poieo as well.
verses.notes: Confirms Christ as Creator. 'By him were all things created' and 'by him all things consist.' Rev 14:7's 'worship him that made' implicitly invokes this christological reality.

verses.heading: Fourth commandment — the creation formula's primary allusion target
verses.kjv_text: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days shalt thou labour, and do all thy work: But the seventh day [is] the sabbath of the LORD thy God: [in it] thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter, thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thy cattle, nor thy stranger that [is] within thy gates: For [in] six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them [is], and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
verses.context: The fourth of the Ten Commandments. Exo 20:11 provides the creation formula that Rev 14:7 alludes to: 'made heaven and earth, the sea.'
verses.notes: Four terms match Rev 14:7 in the same sequence: made (asah/poieo), heaven (shamayim/ouranon), earth (erets/gen), sea (yam/thalassan). Rev 14:7 diverges with 'fountains of waters' instead of 'all that in them is.' The verb asah (H6213) maps to poieo (G4160) in the LXX — the same verb Rev 14:7 uses instead of ktizo. Paulien: 'There is no direct allusion to the Old Testament in Revelation that is more certain than the allusion to the fourth commandment in Rev 14:7.' The Sabbath is the only commandment containing the three-element seal: name (YHWH), title (Creator), territory (heaven, earth, sea).

verses.heading: Heavenly worship of Creator using ktizo — contrast with Rev 14:7's poieo
verses.kjv_text: Thou art worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honour and power: for thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created.
verses.context: The throne-room worship scene in Revelation 4. The twenty-four elders worship the Creator using ktizo (ektisas, ektisthesan).
verses.notes: Uses ektisas (G2936, ktizo) — NOT poieo. Revelation uses ktizo for generic creation praise in 4:11 and 10:6. Only 14:7 uses poieo — deliberately chosen to alluse to Exo 20:11 LXX.

verses.heading: Angel's oath by Creator using ktizo — Rev 14:7 deliberately shifts to poieo
verses.kjv_text: And sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven, and the things that therein are, and the earth, and the things that therein are, and the sea, and the things which are therein, that there should be time no longer:
verses.context: The mighty angel's oath formula. Lists the same creation domains (heaven, earth, sea) but uses ktizo (ektisen) and includes 'the things therein.'
verses.notes: Uses ektisen (G2936, ktizo). The creation domain list is nearly identical to Rev 14:7 (heaven, earth, sea) but uses the generic creation verb. Rev 14:7's switch to poieo is deliberate — it invokes the specific vocabulary of Exo 20:11 LXX.

verses.heading: The glory-exchange failure — exact antiparallel to Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness; Because that which may be known of God is manifest in them; for God hath shewed [it] unto them. For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, [even] his eternal power and Godhead; so that they are without excuse: Because that, when they knew God, they glorified [him] not as God, neither were thankful; but became vain in their imaginations, and their foolish heart was darkened. Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools, And changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things.
verses.context: Romans 1:18-23 describes humanity's failure to glorify God despite knowing Him through creation. This is the precise antiparallel to the first angel's command.
verses.notes: Rom 1:21 — edoxasan (G1392, aorist active ind) — 'they glorified [him] not as God.' The first angel's 'give him glory' (dote auto doxan) is the exact reversal. The glory-exchange of Rom 1:23 (using eikon, G1504 — the same root word as Rev 13's 'image of the beast') shows the first angel reversing the primal human failure: failure to glorify leads to idolatry, which the first angel corrects by calling people to worship the Creator.

verses.heading: Abraham's faith IS glory-giving — same construction as Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: He staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief; but was strong in faith, giving glory to God;
verses.context: Paul's exposition of Abraham's faith in Romans 4. Abraham's faith is expressed as glory-giving.
verses.notes: dous doxan to Theo (giving glory to God) — aorist active participle of didomi (G1325) + doxa (G1391) + dative. Same construction as dote auto doxan in Rev 14:7 (didomi + doxa + dative). Abraham's faith IS his glory-giving — not works-righteousness but faith expressed as recognition of God's reality and power.

verses.heading: Verbal echo of Rev 14:7 — same three lexemes, but refusal
verses.kjv_text: And men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues: and they repented not to give him glory.
verses.context: The fourth bowl plague. Those under judgment refuse to repent and give God glory — the exact opposite of what the first angel commanded.
verses.notes: Same three Greek lexemes as Rev 14:7: didomi + autos + doxa. In 14:7 it is a command (dote auto doxan, 'give him glory'). In 16:9 it is a refusal (ou metenoesan dounai auto doxan, 'they repented not to give him glory'). The gospel call rejected becomes the standard of judgment.

verses.heading: 'Give glory to God' as confession/truth-telling before judgment
verses.kjv_text: And Joshua said unto Achan, My son, give, I pray thee, glory to the LORD God of Israel, and make confession unto him; and tell me now what thou hast done; hide [it] not from me.
verses.context: After Achan's theft of devoted things at Jericho. Joshua calls Achan to 'give glory to the LORD' — meaning confession and truth-telling before God's judgment seat.
verses.notes: The Hebraic expression 'give glory to God' means 'tell the truth before God's judgment seat.' This connects the glory imperative (Rev 14:7) directly to the judgment announcement — fear God (internal orientation), give glory (confess truth/acknowledge God's character), because judgment is coming.

verses.heading: Throne-room judgment scene — the event the first angel announces
verses.kjv_text: I beheld till the thrones were cast down, and the Ancient of days did sit, whose garment [was] white as snow, and the hair of his head like the pure wool: his throne [was like] the fiery flame, [and] his wheels [as] burning fire. A fiery stream issued and came forth from before him: thousand thousands ministered unto him, and ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him: the judgment was set, and the books were opened.
verses.context: Daniel's throne-room vision of the pre-advent judgment. This is the event the first angel announces has come.
verses.notes: The Aramaic dina (judgment) connects via LXX kriterion/krima to NT krisis. The books being opened establishes the investigative/evaluative nature of this judgment — krisis (process), not katakrima (condemnation).

verses.heading: Judgment given FOR the saints — benefactive le preposition
verses.kjv_text: Until the Ancient of days came, and judgment was given to the saints of the most High; and the time came that the saints possessed the kingdom.
verses.context: The outcome of the throne-room judgment: it results in the saints receiving the kingdom. Judgment is FOR, not AGAINST, God's people.
verses.notes: Aramaic: dina yehib leqaddishe elyonin — 'judgment was given FOR the saints.' The preposition le is benefactive. The judgment reverses persecution, not creates it. This is why judgment IS gospel — it vindicates the faithful.

verses.heading: Compressed gospel summary containing all first angel elements
verses.kjv_text: For they themselves shew of us what manner of entering in we had unto you, and how ye turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God; And to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, [even] Jesus, which delivered us from the wrath to come.
verses.context: Paul's summary of the Thessalonians' conversion. The most compressed parallel to the first angel's message in one sentence.
verses.notes: Element mapping: (1) 'turned from idols' = worship correction (Rev 14:7 worship the Creator, not the beast); (2) 'serve the living and true God' = fear God and give glory (Rev 14:7); (3) 'wait for his Son from heaven' = christological content; (4) 'delivered from the wrath to come' = judgment as gospel assurance. All four elements of the first angel's message compressed into one sentence.

verses.heading: First table of Decalogue — salvation, judgment, and creation themes
verses.kjv_text: I [am] the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage. Thou shalt have no other gods before me. Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness [of any thing] that [is] in heaven above, or that [is] in the earth beneath, or that [is] in the water under the earth: Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God [am] a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth [generation] of them that hate me; And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments.
verses.context: The preamble and first two commandments of the Decalogue. Paulien/Baldwin identify the first angel's message as echoing the First Table: salvation (v.2), judgment (vv.5-6), creation (v.11).
verses.notes: Rev 14:6-7 echoes the First Table: (1) euangelion aionion (salvation, cf. Exo 20:2 'brought thee out'); (2) 'the hour of his judgment' (cf. Exo 20:5-6 visiting iniquity/showing mercy); (3) 'him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea' (cf. Exo 20:11). Paulien: 'a triple collection of thematic references' virtually confirming the fourth commandment allusion.

verses.heading: Fear God not man — the fear that grounds resistance to the beast
verses.kjv_text: And fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to destroy both soul and body in hell.
verses.context: Jesus instructs disciples on persecution. The one who fears God cannot be moved by human threats.
verses.notes: The person who has settled whom they ultimately fear (phobeo, same verb as Rev 14:7) cannot be moved by the beast's death threat (Rev 13:15). Fear of God is the foundation for resistance to all counterfeit worship.

verses.heading: Paul condemns substitution for grace, NOT inclusion of judgment/creation in gospel
verses.kjv_text: I marvel that ye are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel: Which is not another; but there be some that trouble you, and would pervert the gospel of Christ. But though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel unto you than that which we have preached unto you, let him be accursed. As we said before, so say I now again, If any [man] preach any other gospel unto you than that ye have received, let him be accursed.
verses.context: Galatians 1 opening. Paul condemns the Judaizers who added circumcision as a salvation condition.
verses.notes: Paul uses heteron (G2087, 'another of different kind') in v.6 and allo (G243, 'another of same kind') in v.7 — 'a different gospel which is not really another.' Paul condemns adding salvation CONDITIONS (circumcision), not adding gospel CONTENT (judgment, creation, commandments) that he himself preached (Rom 2:16, Acts 17:24, 1 Cor 7:19). The Answering Adventism critique misapplies this passage.

verses.heading: Christ's model of glory-giving — completing assigned work
verses.kjv_text: I have glorified thee on the earth: I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do.
verses.context: Jesus' high priestly prayer. He defines his own glory-giving as faithfully completing his assigned work.
verses.notes: Christ's model makes 'give him glory' concrete: glory-giving = faithfully completing assigned work. This is the pattern the saints in Rev 14:12 follow — keeping the commandments and maintaining the faith of Jesus under eschatological pressure.

verses.heading: Sabbatismos — Sabbath-keeping as creation-worship response
verses.kjv_text: There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God.
verses.context: Hebrews 4:1-11 discusses the 'rest' that remains for God's people. The word sabbatismos (G4520, hapax legomenon) specifically means Sabbath-keeping.
verses.notes: Sabbatismos (hapax) means Sabbath-keeping in every extant Greek source. Lincoln (anti-Sabbatarian) concedes the lexical point. Rev 14:7's creation formula invokes the commandment that memorializes creation — the Sabbath. Hebrews 4:9 confirms Sabbath-keeping remains for God's people.

verses.heading: Two fears distinguished in a single verse
verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the people, Fear not: for God is come to prove you, and that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not.
verses.context: Immediately after the Sinai theophany. Moses distinguishes terror-fear ('Fear not' — do not be terrified) from reverential fear ('that his fear may be before your faces').
verses.notes: Two different aspects of fear in one verse: (1) 'Fear not' = do not be terrified (al-tiru, negated imperative); (2) 'that his fear (yirato, H3374) may be before your faces, that ye sin not' = reverential awe as moral orientation. Rev 14:7's phobethete calls for the second kind — the fear that prevents sin, not the fear that produces dread.

verses.heading: Perfect love casts out kolasis-fear, not reverential awe
verses.kjv_text: There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment. He that feareth is not made perfect in love.
verses.context: John's discussion of love and fear. The fear love casts out is specifically kolasis-fear — dread of divine punishment.
verses.notes: The fear (phobos) love casts out is specified by the word kolasis (G2851, punishment/torment — only 2 NT occurrences). This is servile terror, not reverential awe. 1 John 4:18 does NOT contradict Rev 14:7. The first angel calls for reverential fear (the yirah tradition), not the kolasis-fear that love casts out.

verses.heading: Parallel enthronement psalm — new song + judgment of the earth
verses.kjv_text: A Psalm. O sing unto the LORD a new song; for he hath done marvellous things: his right hand, and his holy arm, hath gotten him the victory. The LORD hath made known his salvation: his righteousness hath he openly shewed in the sight of the heathen. He hath remembered his mercy and his truth toward the house of Israel: all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God. Make a joyful noise unto the LORD, all the earth: make a loud noise, and rejoice, and sing praise. Sing unto the LORD with the harp; with the harp, and the voice of a psalm. With trumpets and sound of cornet make a joyful noise before the LORD, the King. Let the sea roar, and the fulness thereof; the world, and they that dwell therein. Let the floods clap [their] hands: let the hills be joyful together Before the LORD; for he cometh to judge the earth: with righteousness shall he judge the world, and the people with equity.
verses.context: Parallel enthronement psalm sharing the structure of Psalm 96 — new song, salvation, universal scope, judgment.
verses.notes: Parallel to Psa 96: new song (v.1), salvation declared (v.2), universal scope (v.3), judgment of the earth (v.9). Confirms the new song + judgment pattern is a recognized OT worship tradition.

verses.heading: New song + creation by word + fear of the LORD — convergence of elements
verses.kjv_text: Rejoice in the LORD, O ye righteous: [for] praise is comely for the upright. Praise the LORD with harp: sing unto him with the psaltery [and] an instrument of ten strings. Sing unto him a new song; play skilfully with a loud noise. For the word of the LORD [is] right; and all his works [are done] in truth. He loveth righteousness and judgment: the earth is full of the goodness of the LORD. By the word of the LORD were the heavens made; and all the host of them by the breath of his mouth. He gathereth the waters of the sea together as an heap: he layeth up the depth in storehouses. Let all the earth fear the LORD: let all the inhabitants of the world stand in awe of him. For he spake, and it was [done]; he commanded, and it stood fast.
verses.context: A praise psalm containing the convergence of new song (v.3), creation (vv.6-9), fear of the LORD (v.8), and judgment/righteousness (v.5).
verses.notes: Combines new song (v.3) + creation by word (v.6) + sea/waters (v.7) + 'let all the earth fear the LORD' (v.8) + righteousness and judgment (v.5). Multiple first angel elements converge in a single psalm.

verses.heading: New song + declare glory among nations
verses.kjv_text: Sing unto the LORD a new song, [and] his praise from the end of the earth, ye that go down to the sea, and all that is therein; the isles, and the inhabitants thereof. Let the wilderness and the cities thereof lift up [their voice], the villages [that] Kedar doth inhabit: let the inhabitants of the rock sing, let them shout from the top of the mountains. Let them give glory unto the LORD, and declare his praise in the islands.
verses.context: Isaiah's new song calling for universal glory-giving. Part of the Servant Songs section.
verses.notes: New song (v.10) + universal scope ('end of the earth,' v.10) + sea (v.10) + 'give glory unto the LORD' (v.12). Direct parallel to both Psa 96 and Rev 14:6-7.

verses.heading: 'Give glory to the LORD your God before he cause darkness' — glory before judgment
verses.kjv_text: Give glory to the LORD your God, before he cause darkness, and before your feet stumble upon the dark mountains, and, while ye look for light, he turn it into the shadow of death, [and] make [it] gross darkness.
verses.context: Jeremiah's call to give God glory BEFORE judgment falls. The urgency matches Rev 14:7's 'for the hour of his judgment IS come.'
verses.notes: The imperative to give glory is linked to the urgency of approaching judgment — the same conjunction as Rev 14:7 where 'give him glory' is grounded by 'for (hoti) the hour of his judgment is come.'

verses.heading: The Lamb's secured victory — immediate literary context preceding the messages
verses.kjv_text: And I looked, and, lo, a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty [and] four thousand, having his Father's name written in their foreheads. And I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps: And they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts, and the elders: and no man could learn that song but the hundred [and] forty [and] four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth. These are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins. These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth. These were redeemed from among men, [being] the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb. And in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God.
verses.context: The literary context immediately preceding the three angels' messages. The Lamb stands victorious with the 144,000, who sing a 'new song' — connecting to Psa 96's new song tradition.
verses.notes: The 'new song' of v.3 links to Psa 96:1's shiyr chadash. If Psa 96 is the OT worship form containing every element of Rev 14:6-7, and the 144,000 sing a new song in the immediate context, the first angel's message is framed by the same worship tradition Psalm 96 represents.

verses.heading: Dual harvest — literary context following the messages
verses.kjv_text: And I looked, and behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud [one] sat like unto the Son of man, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle. And another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap; for the harvest of the earth is ripe. And he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped. And another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle. And another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire; and cried with a loud cry to him that had the sharp sickle, saying, Thrust in thy sharp sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth; for her grapes are fully ripe. And the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast [it] into the great winepress of the wrath of God. And the winepress was trodden without the city, and blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles, by the space of a thousand [and] six hundred furlongs.
verses.context: The dual harvest immediately following the three angels' messages. Two harvests: grain (positive) and grapes (judgment). The messages prepare for this separation.
verses.notes: The three angels' messages are positioned between secured victory (14:1-5) and final harvest (14:14-20). The messages are the call that determines which harvest one belongs to.

verses.heading: Creation vs. idolatry — the LORD made the heavens
verses.kjv_text: For all the gods of the nations [are] idols: but the LORD made the heavens.
verses.context: Psalm 96:5 contrasts the worthless idols of the nations with YHWH who made the heavens. The LXX reads: pantes hoi theoi ton ethnon eidola, ho de kyrios tous ouranous epoiesen — using poieo (same verb as Rev 14:7).
verses.notes: LXX uses epoiesen (poieo) for asah — the same verb Rev 14:7 uses (poiesanti). The creation-vs-idolatry antithesis here is the same dynamic as Acts 17:24-29 and Rev 14:7.

verses.heading: Lystra parallel — turn from vanities to the living God who made heaven, earth, sea
verses.kjv_text: And saying, Sirs, why do ye these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and preach unto you that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God, which made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all things that are therein:
verses.context: Paul and Barnabas at Lystra, redirecting worship from themselves to the Creator God. Uses the same creation formula as Rev 14:7.
verses.notes: Same creation formula: poieo + heaven + earth + sea. Confirms the apostolic pattern of preaching Creator-God worship as gospel content.

verses.heading: Righteous judge — judgment as vindication for the faithful
verses.kjv_text: Henceforth there is laid up for me a crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous judge, shall give me at that day: and not to me only, but unto all them also that love his appearing.
verses.context: Paul near the end of his life anticipates judgment with confidence, not dread. The judge is righteous and the outcome is a crown.
verses.notes: The courtroom has a righteous judge. Judgment is vindication for the faithful — those who love his appearing. Part of the judgment-as-gospel evidence.

verses.heading: Book of remembrance — books of faithful service in judgment
verses.kjv_text: Then they that feared the LORD spake often one to another: and the LORD hearkened, and heard [it], and a book of remembrance was written before him for them that feared the LORD, and that thought upon his name. And they shall be mine, saith the LORD of hosts, in that day when I make up my jewels; and I will spare them, as a man spareth his own son that serveth him.
verses.context: Malachi describes God keeping a book of remembrance for those who fear Him. The books in judgment (Dan 7:10) include records of faithful service.
verses.notes: Books of faithful service in judgment — not only books of sins but books recording faithfulness. Those who feared the LORD are claimed as God's jewels. Judgment includes vindication.

verses.heading: Ark of the covenant opened — activating the covenant-law theme
verses.kjv_text: And the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament: and there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail.
verses.context: The ark of the covenant is revealed in heaven's temple, activating the covenant-law theme that runs through Rev 12:17 to 14:12.
verses.notes: The ark contains the Ten Commandments. Its opening in heaven activates the covenant-law theme for the entire sequence through Rev 12:17 (commandments of God) to Rev 14:12 (commandments of God). The commandment inclusio is grounded here.

verses.heading: Sabbath as sign (owth) — first of four sign references
verses.kjv_text: Speak thou also unto the children of Israel, saying, Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it [is] a sign between me and you throughout your generations; that [ye] may know that I [am] the LORD that doth sanctify you.
verses.context: God designates the Sabbath as a sign (owth) between Himself and Israel. First of four owth references for the Sabbath.
verses.notes: Sabbath as owth (sign, H226) — the only commandment explicitly called a sign. Four times: Exo 31:13, 31:17, Eze 20:12, 20:20.

verses.heading: Sabbath as sign — creation connection
verses.kjv_text: It [is] a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for [in] six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed.
verses.context: Second owth reference. Explicitly connects the Sabbath sign to creation — the same creation act Rev 14:7 invokes.
verses.notes: Connects the Sabbath sign directly to creation: the LORD made (asah) heaven and earth. Same creation vocabulary as Exo 20:11 and Rev 14:7.

verses.heading: Sabbath as sign of sanctification
verses.kjv_text: Moreover also I gave them my sabbaths, to be a sign between me and them, that they might know that I [am] the LORD that sanctify them.
verses.context: Third owth reference. The Sabbath sign identifies YHWH as the one who sanctifies.
verses.notes: Sabbath as sign of sanctification. The Creator who is worshipped (Rev 14:7) is also the Sanctifier.

verses.heading: Apostolic quotation of Isa 52:7 — basar/euangelizo bridge
verses.kjv_text: And how shall they preach, except they be sent? as it is written, How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace, and bring glad tidings of good things!
verses.context: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:7, bridging the Hebrew basar vocabulary to the Greek euangelizo tradition.
verses.notes: Confirms the basar-euangelizo vocabulary chain by apostolic quotation. The Hebrew herald tradition (Isa 52:7) maps directly to NT gospel proclamation.

verses.heading: proeuangelizomai — God preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham
verses.kjv_text: And the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, [saying], In thee shall all nations be blessed.
verses.context: Paul coins proeuangelizomai (hapax) to describe God preaching the gospel to Abraham before the NT era.
verses.notes: The gospel of Rev 14:6 is not new — it is aionios (everlasting), the same gospel preached beforehand (pro-euangelizo) to Abraham. Confirms the eternal scope of euangelion aionion.

verses.heading: Beholding-transformation — glory-giving through contemplation of Christ
verses.kjv_text: But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, [even] as by the Spirit of the Lord.
verses.context: Paul describes the transformation process: beholding the Lord's glory produces progressive transformation into that same glory.
verses.notes: One of seven modes of glory-giving identified in Study 06: beholding-transformation. Glory-giving is not merely verbal acknowledgment but a transformative process.

verses.heading: Glorify your Father — visible conduct as mode of glory-giving
verses.kjv_text: Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.
verses.context: Jesus teaches that visible righteous conduct causes others to glorify God. A mode of glory-giving.
verses.notes: Visible conduct as glory-giving. The first angel's command to give God glory includes this dimension — living in a way that makes God's character visible.

verses.heading: Glorify God in your body — bodily stewardship as glory-giving
verses.kjv_text: For ye are bought with a price: therefore glorify God in your body, and in your spirit, which are God's.
verses.context: Paul connects bodily stewardship to glorifying God. The body is God's, purchased by Christ.
verses.notes: Bodily stewardship as glory-giving. The first angel's dote doxan includes the physical dimension of honoring God with one's body.

verses.heading: Do all to the glory of God — comprehensive glory-giving
verses.kjv_text: Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God.
verses.context: Paul makes glory-giving comprehensive — every activity, including eating and drinking, should be done to God's glory.
verses.notes: The most comprehensive glory-giving command in the NT. The first angel's dote doxan encompasses all of life, not just worship services.

verses.heading: Death threat from the beast — fear of man vs. fear of God
verses.kjv_text: And he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak, and cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed.
verses.context: The beast power threatens death to those who refuse to worship its image. This is the threat that the first angel's fear-of-God command must overcome.
verses.notes: The person who has settled whom they ultimately fear (phobethete ton theon, Rev 14:7) cannot be moved by the beast's death threat (Matt 10:28). The first angel's message directly prepares believers for the beast's coercion.

verses.heading: Heavens declare the glory of God — creation-revelation precedent
verses.kjv_text: To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David. The heavens declare the glory of God; and the firmament sheweth his handywork. Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night sheweth knowledge. [There is] no speech nor language, [where] their voice is not heard. Their line is gone out through all the earth, and their words to the end of the world. In them hath he set a tabernacle for the sun, Which [is] as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber, [and] rejoiceth as a strong man to run a race. His going forth [is] from the end of the heaven, and his circuit unto the ends of it: and there is nothing hid from the heat thereof.
verses.context: David declares that creation itself proclaims God's glory. The heavens are a universal witness requiring no human language.
verses.notes: Creation as glory-proclamation. The heavens declare kabod (H3519) — the same glory the first angel commands humanity to give. Creation's witness is universal (v.4 'through all the earth'), paralleling Rev 14:6's universal scope.

verses.heading: Climax of Athens sermon — appointed day, judge the world, proof by resurrection
verses.kjv_text: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by [that] man whom he hath ordained; [whereof] he hath given assurance unto all [men], in that he hath raised him from the dead.
verses.context: The climax of Paul's Athens sermon. He moves from creation (v.24) through worship correction (vv.25-29) to judgment (v.31), finally introducing Christ through the resurrection.
verses.notes: Temporal shift: mellei krinein ('is about to judge', present of mello + infinitive) vs. Rev 14:7's elthen ('IS come', prophetic aorist). Same message, different temporal phase. Paul delays mentioning Christ until this climax — the resurrection as proof (pistin paraschon). If Paul's speech is gospel with delayed christological naming, Rev 14:7 follows the same pattern.

verses.heading: God proclaiming His own glory/character — definition of kabod-doxa content
verses.kjv_text: And the LORD passed by before him, and proclaimed, The LORD, The LORD God, merciful and gracious, longsuffering, and abundant in goodness and truth, Keeping mercy for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, and that will by no means clear [the guilty]; visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children, and upon the children's children, unto the third and to the fourth [generation].
verses.context: God's self-proclamation of His character in response to Moses' request to see His glory (Exo 33:18). This defines what kabod/doxa actually IS.
verses.notes: When Moses asked to see God's glory (kabod, 33:18), God proclaimed His CHARACTER — merciful, gracious, longsuffering, forgiving, yet just. Glory-giving (Rev 14:7 dote doxan) means recognizing THIS character, not merely visible radiance.

verses.heading: God as judge — he cometh and shall not keep silence
verses.kjv_text: Our God shall come, and shall not keep silence: a fire shall devour before him, and it shall be very tempestuous round about him. He shall call to the heavens from above, and to the earth, that he may judge his people. Gather my saints together unto me; those that have made a covenant with me by sacrifice. And the heavens shall declare his righteousness: for God [is] judge himself. Selah.
verses.context: A judgment theophany psalm. God comes to judge His own covenant people — not outsiders but those who made covenant with Him.
verses.notes: God judges His own covenant people (v.4-5). The judgment is righteous (v.6). The heavens declare His righteousness in judgment. Parallels the first angel's announcement of judgment to those who have the everlasting gospel.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'good message/gospel.' From eu (good) + angello (announce). 77 tokens in 74 verses across 17 books. Virtually uniformly translated 'gospel' in KJV (94.6%). Concentrated in Pauline epistles (Romans 10, 2 Corinthians 8, Galatians 7). The noun denotes content of proclamation rather than the act of proclaiming.
word_studies.relevance: The text labels the first angel's content as euangelion aionion. The anarthrous construction is qualitative ('gospel of an eternal kind'), not indefinite. The cognate accusative (euangelion euangelisai) intensifies the gospel-ness.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to announce good news, evangelize.' 52 unique verses (61 tokens) across 12 books. Overwhelmingly middle voice (88%) — the evangelist personally engages as an invested participant. Present tense dominant (25), followed by aorist (20).
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:6 is the only NT verse where the noun euangelion and verb euangelisai co-occur — a cognate accusative intensifying the gospel character. Luke 3:18 uses euangelizeto to label John's judgment preaching as gospel.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to fear, be afraid, revere.' 90 unique verses (110 tokens) across 14 books. Spans three semantic domains: fear/terror (025), worship/reverence (053), and honor/status (087). Nearly all forms are middle/passive voice — deponent. The aorist imperative phobethete in Rev 14:7 is a decisive call to reverential awe.
word_studies.relevance: First imperative of the triad. Bridges to H3372 yare in the OT. The aorist imperative form calls for a decisive, specific act — not ongoing habit but definitive commitment to fear God.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'fear, terror, reverence.' 44 unique verses (47 tokens) across 16 books. Spans fear/terror and worship/reverence domains. 77.3% translated 'fear' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: The noun form of the first angel's imperative. 1 John 4:18 specifies that love casts out kolasis-fear (dread of punishment), not phobos in its reverential sense.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'glory, honor, splendor.' 151 unique verses (168 tokens) across 23 books. Root shifted from classical Greek 'opinion/reputation' to LXX sense of visible, luminous majesty — particularly the manifest presence of God (Hebrew kabod). Carries both visible-radiance and relational honor senses.
word_studies.relevance: Second imperative object — 'give him glory.' The didomi + doxa + dative construction appears in Rev 14:7, Rom 4:20, and Rev 16:9 (the refusal echo). LXX equivalent of kabod (H3519).

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to glorify, render glorious, honor.' 54 unique verses (71 tokens) across 13 books. Spans praise, honor/status, and value domains.
word_studies.relevance: Rom 1:21's edoxasan (aorist active) with negative — 'they glorified him NOT as God' — is the exact failure the first angel's dote doxan reverses.

word_studies.summary: Primary verb meaning 'to give' with extraordinarily wide application. 377 unique verses (430 tokens) across 22 books. Spans giving, lending, dedicating, and assigning domains. Aorist dominant (59%).
word_studies.relevance: The verb in 'give him glory' (dote auto doxan). Same verb in the Rev 16:9 refusal echo (dounai auto doxan) and Rom 4:20 (dous doxan to theo). The didomi + doxa + dative construction links Rev 14:7, Rom 4:20, and Rev 16:9.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to worship, prostrate, do homage.' 54 unique verses (65 tokens). From pros (toward) + probable derivative of kyon (dog — kissing the hand). LXX equivalent of shachah (H7812). Almost exclusively translated 'worship' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: Third imperative — the behavioral expression of the triad. The worship contest between God and the beast (Rev 13:15 vs 14:7) makes proskyneo the central issue of the three angels' messages.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to make, do.' 518 unique verses (597 tokens) across 26 books — the broadest Greek action verb. 50% translated 'do/did,' 15% 'make/made.' The LXX equivalent of asah (H6213).
word_studies.relevance: Deliberately chosen over ktizo in Rev 14:7 to invoke Exo 20:11 LXX (asah = poieo). Rev 4:11 and 10:6 use ktizo; only 14:7 uses poieo. Acts 17:24 also uses poieo (poiesas) — confirming the creation-verb parallel.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to create, found, form originally.' Only 12 unique verses (16 tokens). Technical creation vocabulary — more specialized than poieo. Used in Rev 4:11 (ektisas, ektisthesan) and Rev 10:6 (ektisen) but NOT in Rev 14:7.
word_studies.relevance: The verb Revelation does NOT use in 14:7. The deliberate switch from ktizo (Rev 4:11, 10:6) to poieo (Rev 14:7) is the strongest evidence for the Exo 20:11 allusion. Mueller (BRI): 'By using poieo instead of ktizo, the connection to Genesis 1-2 is even more clearly established.'

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'judgment, judicial process.' 47 NT occurrences. From krino (to separate, judge). Spans court of justice, judging activity, evaluation, and punishment domains. 55% translated 'judgment' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: The PROCESS word — the angel chose krisis (47 NT occurrences, judicial evaluation) over katakrima (3 occurrences, adverse verdict + execution). This linguistic choice shapes the entire theology of Rev 14:7: judgment is investigation, not condemnation.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'condemnation, adverse sentence.' Only 3 NT occurrences, all in Romans (5:16, 5:18, 8:1). From kata (against) + krima (verdict). 100% translated 'condemnation' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: The word the angel did NOT choose. Rev 14:7 uses krisis (process), not katakrima (condemnation). Rom 8:1 negates katakrima for believers: 'There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus.' Judgment IS gospel because it is krisis, not katakrima.

word_studies.summary: Noun meaning 'hour, time, season.' 108 occurrences. 70.4% translated 'hour.' Concentrated in Gospels (John 26, Matthew 23, Luke 16, Mark 12) and Revelation (10).
word_studies.relevance: The eschatological timing marker: 'the hour of his judgment IS come.' The articular construction (he hora) makes this a specific, appointed hour — not a general time but THE hour.

word_studies.summary: Adjective meaning 'eternal, everlasting, perpetual.' 71 tokens in 69 verses across 19 books. From aion (G165). 92% rendered 'eternal' or 'everlasting' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: Qualifies the gospel as 'everlasting' — not a new gospel but the same gospel proclaimed from the beginning (Gal 3:8 proeuangelizomai). The anarthrous construction with euangelion is qualitative: 'gospel of an eternal kind.'

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'faith, trust, persuasion.' 244 occurrences. From peitho (to persuade). Spans belief, trust, and promise domains. 68.9% translated 'faith' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:12's 'faith of Jesus' (pistin Iesou) — subjective genitive ('the faithfulness OF Jesus') or objective genitive ('faith IN Jesus'). Both work; the subjective genitive adds christological depth: the saints exhibit Christ's own faithfulness.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'commandment, precept.' 65 unique verses (71 tokens). From entellomai (to command). 93.8% translated 'commandment(s)' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: The commandment inclusio: Rev 12:17 (entolas tou theou) and Rev 14:12 (entolas tou theou) bracket the three angels' messages. In a passage concluding with 'commandments of God,' the creation formula's strongest link is to the commandment memorializing creation.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to repent, change one's mind.' 32 unique verses (36 tokens). From meta (after/change) + noeo (to think). Fundamentally cognitive-volitional — deliberate reorientation of thought and will, not merely emotional remorse. Concentrated in Revelation (10), Luke (9), Matthew (5).
word_studies.relevance: The Acts 17:30 imperative ('commands all people everywhere to repent') parallels Rev 14:7's 'fear God and give him glory.' Repentance and fear-of-God are two angles on the same moral reorientation — metanoeo is the Athenian equivalent of phobethete.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to keep, guard, watch over.' 75 occurrences. From teros (a watch). Differs from phylasso (preventing escape) — tereo implies keeping the eye upon, noting, fulfilling. 17.3% translated 'keep,' 12% 'keepeth.'
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:12's hoi terountes (present active participle) is substantival — describing who the saints ARE. The word implies watchful, careful guarding of commandments, not mere external compliance.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'patience, endurance, steadfast perseverance.' 32 occurrences. From hupo (under) + meno (remain). Pictures bearing a weight from below — active endurance, not passive resignation. 78.1% translated 'patience' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:12: 'Here is the patience (hupomone) of the saints.' The hupomone inclusio (Rev 13:10 to 14:12) brackets the three angels' messages as a single literary unit. Active endurance under eschatological pressure, not passive waiting.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to judge, distinguish, decide.' 98 unique verses (132 tokens) across 15 books. Primary root behind the krisis word family. Active voice dominates (74%), present tense most frequent (54%).
word_studies.relevance: Acts 17:31 uses krinein (present active infinitive of krino) for the future judgment Paul announces. Same root as krisis in Rev 14:7.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root verb meaning 'to fear, revere.' 317 unique verses (414 tokens) across 34 books. Qal dominant (85%) covering both natural fear and reverent awe. Niphal participle nora means 'awesome, terrible' applied to God.
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew root behind phobeo. The farewell-address pattern traces yare through Moses (Deut 10:12), Joshua (Josh 24:14), Samuel (1 Sam 12:24), Solomon (Eccl 12:13), and the first angel (Rev 14:7). LXX yare maps to phobeo with 241 co-occurrences.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'glory, weight, honor.' 189 unique verses (214 tokens) in 27 books. From kabad (to be heavy). Root idea is literal heaviness applied metaphorically — substance, importance, radiance. 82% translated 'glory' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew background for doxa. Psa 96:7-8 'give unto the LORD glory (kabod)' maps to Rev 14:7 'give him glory (doxa).' When Moses asked to see God's glory (kabod, Exo 33:18), God proclaimed His character (Exo 34:6-7) — glory-giving = recognizing God's full character-weight.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'commandment, charge.' 177 unique verses (190 tokens), 23 books. From tsavah (to command). 98%+ translated 'commandment(s)' in KJV. Concentrated in Deuteronomy (42).
word_studies.relevance: Eccl 12:13's mitsvotav shamar ('keep his commandments') maps to Rev 14:12's entolas tou theou ('commandments of God'). LXX mitsvah maps to entole with 153 co-occurrences.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'judgment, justice, ordinance, right.' 406 unique verses (448 tokens) across 31 books. From shaphat (to judge). Properly: a verdict (favorable or unfavorable) — covers the act, the place, the suit, the crime, and the penalty. 72% translated 'judgment/judgments' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: Eccl 12:14's mishpat maps to Rev 14:7's krisis. Both are process words — the judicial evaluation, not the condemnation sentence.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root verb meaning 'to do, make.' 2,283 unique verses (2,934 tokens) — one of the most frequent OT verbs. Present in every OT book. The semantic default all-purpose action verb. 39% 'do/did,' 21% 'make/made.'
word_studies.relevance: Exo 20:11: 'the LORD made (asah) heaven and earth, the sea.' LXX translates asah as poieo. Rev 14:7 uses poieo (poiesanti) rather than ktizo, mapping to asah via the LXX. Psa 96:5: 'the LORD made (asah) the heavens' — same verb in the same creation-worship context.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root verb meaning 'to keep, guard, watch over.' Etymologically 'to hedge about (as with thorns).' 408 verbal tokens. Qal dominant (367). 55% translated 'keep/kept/keeper.'
word_studies.relevance: Eccl 12:13's shamar ('keep his commandments') is the covenant-keeping verb. From the first command to Adam (Gen 2:15 'to keep it') to Solomon's conclusion. Maps to tereo (G5083) in the NT, the verb in Rev 14:12.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root verb meaning 'to judge, govern, vindicate.' 182 unique verses (223 tokens) in 31 books. Qal dominant (184). 59% translated 'judge/judged/judging.' Definition includes 'to vindicate or punish' — the verb can be for or against.
word_studies.relevance: Psa 96:13 uses shaphat twice: 'he cometh to judge (lishpot, Qal InfCon) the earth: he shall judge (yishpot, Qal Impf) the world.' This is the OT equivalent of Rev 14:7's krisis announcement.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to proclaim good news, announce glad tidings.' 21 unique verses (27 tokens) across 8 books. Overwhelmingly Piel (23/27) — intensive active proclamation. The Hebrew root behind euangelizo.
word_studies.relevance: Psa 96:2 uses basseru (Piel imperative): 'proclaim good news of his salvation from day to day.' This is literally the OT euangelizo. The basar-euangelizo vocabulary chain is confirmed by apostolic quotation (Rom 10:15 quoting Isa 52:7) and dominical application (Luk 4:18 quoting Isa 61:1).

word_studies.summary: Adjective meaning 'new, fresh.' 48 unique verses (53 tokens) across 20 books. From chadash (verb, to be new, renew). 75% translated 'new' in KJV.
word_studies.relevance: Psa 96:1's shiyr chadash ('new song') connects to Rev 14:3's 'new song' through the LXX ode kaine. The new song tradition (Psa 33:3, 40:3, 96:1, 98:1, 144:9, 149:1, Isa 42:10, Rev 5:9, Rev 14:3) frames the first angel's message in the OT worship tradition.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root meaning 'to bow down, prostrate, worship.' 166 unique verses (194 tokens) across 25 books. Hishtaphel dominant (99%) — reflexive prostration. 46% 'worship,' 29% 'bowed.'
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew behind proskyneo. Psa 96:9: 'O worship (hishtachwu) the LORD in the beauty of holiness' — the same worship call as Rev 14:7's proskynesate.

word_studies.summary: Noun meaning 'man, mankind, human.' The word in Eccl 12:13's kol-ha-adam ('the whole of man'). Three interpretive options for kol-ha-adam: (1) 'all mankind' — universal scope, (2) 'whole duty of man' — KJV adds 'duty' in italics, (3) 'man's all' — the totality of human purpose/being.
word_studies.relevance: The kol-ha-adam phrase defines what the first angel's message IS — if it means 'man's all' (entire purpose), then Eccl 12:13 says the fear-commandments-judgment triad IS the totality of human existence. This parallels euangelion aionion as the comprehensive message.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'proclamation, preaching, what is preached.' 8 unique verses. From kerysso (to herald). The formal public proclamation — the content delivered by a herald.
word_studies.relevance: The first angel's message is kerygma — a formal herald's proclamation with authority. The angel acts as God's herald proclaiming the euangelion.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to announce glad news in advance, preach the gospel beforehand.' HAPAX LEGOMENON — appears only in Gal 3:8. From pro (before) + euangelizo.
word_studies.relevance: God 'preached the gospel beforehand' (proeuangelisato) to Abraham. The gospel of Rev 14:6 is not new — it is the same message proclaimed since Abraham. Confirms 'everlasting' (aionios) gospel.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to say, speak.' The most common speech-introducing verb in the NT. The legon participle in Rev 14:7 is the present active nominative masculine singular form.
word_studies.relevance: The legon participle structurally embeds v.7 as the CONTENT of the euangelion in v.6. See grammar reference entry for full analysis.

parallels.connection_type: structural + vocabulary — fear God (yare/phobeo) + commandments (mitsvah/entole) + judgment (mishpat/krisis). The OT structural twin.
structural + vocabulary — Creator (poieo in both), universal scope, judgment (krino/krisis), worship correction. The apostolic precedent.
structural + vocabulary — new song, basar/euangelizo, glory (kabod/doxa), creation (asah/poieo), judgment (shaphat/krisis). Every element present.
vocabulary — four terms in identical sequence: made (asah/poieo), heaven (shamayim/ouranos), earth (erets/ge), sea (yam/thalassa). The most certain OT allusion in Revelation.

antiparallel — Rom 1:21 'glorified him NOT as God' is the exact failure Rev 14:7 'give him glory' reverses. The glory-exchange (eikon, same root as Rev 13's image) is the antiparallel of Creator-worship.
verbal echo — same three lexemes (didomi + autos + doxa). Command in 14:7 becomes refused purpose in 16:9.
vocabulary — dous doxan to Theo (giving glory to God) = same construction as dote auto doxan in Rev 14:7. Abraham's faith IS glory-giving.
pattern — first iteration of farewell-address pattern: fear + commandments + serve.
pattern — second iteration of farewell-address pattern: fear + serve + remove idols.
pattern — third iteration of farewell-address pattern: fear + serve + remember God's acts.
thematic — Paul labels judgment as 'according to my gospel' (kata to euangelion mou). Judgment IS gospel content.
thematic — Luke labels John's judgment preaching as euangelizeto. Judgment preaching IS gospel.
thematic — the throne-room judgment scene the first angel announces. Aramaic dina connects via LXX to krisis.
thematic — judgment given FOR the saints (benefactive le). Judgment vindicates the faithful.
structural — compressed gospel containing all first angel elements in one sentence: turned from idols + serve living God + wait for Son + delivered from wrath.
christological — Christ as Creator. 'Him that made' in Rev 14:7 implicitly invokes Christ as the agent of creation.
christological — 'by him were all things created.' Christ IS the Creator invoked by Rev 14:7.
vocabulary + structural — 'turn from vanities unto the living God, which made (poieo) heaven, and earth, and the sea.' Same creation formula, same verb, same elements.
vocabulary — shiyr chadash (new song) connects to Rev 14:3's 'new song' through the LXX ode kaine. The worship tradition framing the first angel's message.
vocabulary — basseru (Piel of basar, 'proclaim good news') is the Hebrew root of euangelizo. The psalm literally says 'proclaim good news of his salvation.'
inclusio — both use entolas tou theou. Commandment-keeping brackets the three angels' messages.
pattern — Abraham believed (faith FIRST), then obeyed (Gen 26:5, obedience as FRUIT). Rev 14:12 presents the same pattern under eschatological pressure.
vocabulary + thematic — 'give glory to the LORD God of Israel and make confession.' Hebraic expression for truth-telling before God's judgment seat.
vocabulary + thematic — 'Give glory to the LORD your God, before he cause darkness.' Glory-giving before judgment — same urgency as Rev 14:7.
structural — parallel enthronement psalm with new song (v.1) and judgment (v.9). Confirms the worship pattern.
structural — new song (v.3) + creation by word (v.6) + fear of the LORD (v.8). Multiple first angel elements converge.
structural — new song (v.10) + universal scope + 'give glory unto the LORD' (v.12). Direct parallel.

Key forms: hupomone (G5281 N-NSF), terountes (G5083 V-PAP-NPM substantival present active participle), entolas (G1785 N-APF), Theou (G2316 N-GSM), pistin (G4102 N-ASF), Iesou (G2424 N-GSM genitive).

Key forms: poiesas (G4160 V-AAP-NSM aorist active participle nominative — SAME VERB poieo as Rev 14:7), kosmon (G2889 N-ASM), ouranou (G3772 N-GSM), ges (G1093 N-GSF), cheiropoietois (G5499 A-DPM 'made with hands'), naois (G3485 N-DPM 'temples').

Key forms: apaggellei (G518 V-PAI-3S 'commands/declares'), pantas (G3956 A-APM 'all' — universal scope), pantachou (G3837 ADV 'everywhere' — universal scope), metanoein (G3340 V-PAN present active infinitive 'to repent').

Key forms: mellei (G3195 V-PAI-3S 'is about to' — future, CONTRAST with elthen in Rev 14:7), krinein (G2919 V-PAN present active infinitive 'to judge'), oikoumenen (G3625 N-ASF 'the inhabited world'), dikaiosyne (G1343 N-DSF 'righteousness'), anastesas (G450 V-AAP-NSM 'having raised' — resurrection as proof).

Key forms: edoxasan (G1392 V-AAI-3P aorist active indicative 'they glorified' — with ouch = 'they did NOT glorify'), emataiothesen (G3154 V-API-3P 'became vain'), eskotisthe (G4654 V-API-3S 'was darkened').

Key forms: krinei (G2919 V-PAI-3S 'judges'), euangelion (G2098 N-ASN articular 'THE gospel'), mou (G1473 P-1GS 'my' — Paul labels judgment as 'according to MY gospel'), Christou Iesou (G5547+G2424 genitive 'through Christ Jesus').

Key forms: dous (G1325 V-2AAP-NSM aorist active participle 'giving'), doxan (G1391 N-ASF 'glory'), to Theo (G2316 N-DSM dative 'to God'). SAME CONSTRUCTION as Rev 14:7 dote auto doxan (didomi + doxa + dative).
